content.problemStatement:
You are given a list of intervals, where each interval is represented as a pair of integers [start,end][start, end] [start,end]. Your task is to merge all overlapping intervals and return a list of non-overlapping intervals that cover all the intervals in the input.

The intervals will be provided as a 2D collection (e.g., list of lists, array of arrays, vector of vectors), depending on the language. Please look at the function parameter provided in the coding area for this.

**Notes:**


Intervals may be given in any order.
Intervals that touch at the endpoints (e.g., [1,4][1,4][1,4] and [4,5][4,5][4,5]) are considered overlapping.
The output intervals should be sorted by their start values.
The solution should return a 2D collection of merged intervals.
Function DeclarationFunction Namemergemergemerge – This function merges overlapping intervals and returns the merged result.

Parameters
intervalsintervalsintervals : A 2D collection where each element is a pair [start,end][start, end][start,end] representing an interval.
Return Value
Returns a 2D array collection of merged, non-overlapping intervals sorted by start time.
Constraints
0≤T≤1000 \leq T \leq 1000≤T≤100
1≤numberofintervalspertestcase≤1051 \leq number of intervals per test case \leq 10^51≤numberofintervalspertestcase≤105
0≤start≤end≤1050 \leq start \leq end \leq 10^50≤start≤end≤105
Intervals may overlap or be disjoint

Input Format


The first line of input contains a single integer TTT — the number of test cases.




Each test case consists of multiple lines of input.



The first line of each test case contains a single integer NNN — the number of intervals.
The next NNN lines each contain two space-separated integers startstartstart and endendend, denoting one interval.




Output Format


For each test case, print the merged intervals on a single line.




Each interval should be printed in the form:


`[start,end]
`


Intervals should be separated by a space.
content.inputFormat: The first line contains an integer T, the number of test cases. For each test case, the first line contains an integer N, the number of intervals. The next N lines each contain two integers start and end (0 ≤ start ≤ end), representing an interval.
content.outputFormat: For each test case, output a single line containing the merged, non‑overlapping intervals sorted by start time. Each interval is printed as "[start,end]" and intervals are separated by a single space.

sampleTestCases.explanation: Intervals [1,5] and [3,7] overlap and merge to [1,7]. Intervals [8,10] and [9,12] overlap and merge to [8,12].
Test case 1: [1,3] and [2,5] merge into [1,5]. Test case 2: [10,12] and [9,11] merge into [9,12]; [13,15] stays separate.
Only one interval, so the result is the same interval.

solution.approach: For each test case, sort all intervals by their start value (O(N log N)). Then iterate once, maintaining the current merged interval. If the next interval starts <= current end, extend the current end to max(current end, next end). Otherwise, output the current merged interval and start a new one. This greedy merging works because after sorting, any overlapping intervals appear consecutively.
solution.timeComplexity: O(N log N) per test case due to sorting; the merging scan is O(N).
solution.spaceComplexity: O(N) to store the intervals (in‑place sorting can be used, but auxiliary space for the list is O(N)).
